front: Figure: nailed/posted the Ninety-Five Theses to a church door in Wittenberg, sparking the Protestant Reformation
Figure: married the former nun Katharina von Bora, setting a precedent for clerical marriage
Figure: excommunicated/condemned by Pope Leo X after refusing to recant at the Diet of Worms
Figure: called himself a "stout doctor," often depicted in portraits beside the thinner Philip Melanchthon
Figure: his biographer Johannes Mathesius compiled his sayings in the book Table Talk
Document: written after a dispute with the indulgence-seller Johann Tetzel
Document: attacked/criticized the Catholic Church's sale of indulgences
Document: followed the Ninety-Five Theses with A Sermon on Indulgences and Grace, articulating the doctrine of grace, one of the Reformation's five "solas"
back: A Sermon on Indulgences and Grace (Martin Luther)

front: Event: at this 1521 hearing, Luther allegedly declared "Here I stand, I can do no other" when refusing to recant
Event: after this hearing, Luther went into hiding at Wartburg Castle under the alias "Junker Jorg," sheltered by Elector Frederick the Wise of Saxony
Document: condemned the German Peasants' War and rejected the Twelve Articles
back: Against the Murderous, Thieving Hordes of Peasants (Martin Luther)

front: Event: during this war, Luther sided with the magistrates against Anabaptist clergy and their "thieving, murderous hordes" of unruly followers

Event: William Shirer's The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich cites Luther's condemnation of this war as evidence of a "ferocious belief in absolute political authority," tracing a direct link to Hitler
Figure: opposed the Zwickau Prophets, Anabaptists influenced by Thomas Muntzer who believed they received messages directly from God
Concept: in On Temporal Authority, Luther distinguished the coercive government "of the sword" from spiritual government of the divine
Concept: in To the Christian Nobility of the German Nation, Luther called the exclusive papal authority to call a church council the third of "three walls" usurping political authority
back: To the Christian Nobility of the German Nation (Martin Luther)

front: Concept: Luther contrasted mere tyrants with "werewolf" rulers, whom the population had the right to overthrow along with their subordinates
Concept: Luther wrote that a Christian is both a "lord of all" and a "servant of all," and that a believer's whole life should be one of repentance
back: On the Freedom of a Christian (Martin Luther)

front: Event: at this 1519 debate, Luther's ally Andreas Karlstadt argued against the necessity of penance and alms-giving opposite Johann Eck
Event: at this 1529 colloquy, Luther defended sola fide and denied Christ's Real Presence in the Eucharist against Huldrych Zwingli
Concept: in the Heidelberg Disputation, Luther argued that humans have nothing but "empty achievements"
Figure: wanted to remove the Book of James from the Biblical canon for contradicting his doctrine of justification by faith alone
Document: translated the Old Testament into German from Hebrew and drew on Erasmus's Greek New Testament (the Novum Instrumentum omne)
Document: Luther justified its vernacular word choices with the principle "we must ask the mother in the home, the children on the street," in a reply to Jerome Emser
Document: controversially added the word "allein" (alone) to a key passage in Romans, with help from Luther's friend Johannes Bugenhagen
Document: relegated the epistles of James, Jude, and Hebrews toward the end of its canon, calling James an "epistle of straw"
Document: illustrated with 22 woodcuts by Luther's friend Lucas Cranach the Elder
Document: nicknamed the "September Testament" for its first printing in September 1522; introduced iconic alliterative phrases such as "Der Herr ist mein Hirte"
Concept: Max Weber's The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism traces the modern capitalist notion of "vocation" (the German word Beruf) through this translation
Work: a 1529 hymn paraphrasing Psalm 46, containing the line "a bulwark never failing"
back: "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God" (Martin Luther)

front: Figure: apocryphally justified using secular tunes for hymns by asking, "why should the Devil have all the good tunes?"
Work: quoted in the finale of Felix Mendelssohn's Symphony No. 5 ("Reformation")
back: "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God" (Martin Luther)

front: Work: a Christmas carol beginning "Vom Himmel hoch, da komm ich her"
back: "Vom Himmel hoch, da komm ich her" (Martin Luther)

front: Figure: his hymns first appeared in the "Eight Song Book" (Achtliederbuch) alongside Paul Speratus, followed by the Erfurt Enchiridion
Figure: developed the Bar form (AAB structure) chorale often used to close cantatas
Figure: simplified the Te Deum into a version considered his home country's rendering of the hymn
Figure: praised Josquin des Prez as "the master of notes," who "bids the notes as he will" while other composers must obey the notes
Figure: wrote that "next to the Word of God, music deserves the highest praise"
Figure: the forest zither was wrongly named for him by Ferdinand Roese, due to a mistaken belief he was a virtuoso of it
Document: urged the forced conversion or expulsion of Jewish people
back: On the Jews and Their Lies (Martin Luther)
work: On the Jews and Their Lies

front: Document: cited Christ's calling the Pharisees a "brood of vipers" in Matthew, writing "we are at fault for not slaying them"
back: On the Jews and Their Lies (Martin Luther)
work: On the Jews and Their Lies

front: Figure: dismissed the visions of Saint Bridget of Sweden, criticizing her "little prayers"
work: On the Jews and Their Lies

front: Figure: painted repeatedly by his friend Lucas Cranach the Elder, typically in a black robe and floppy hat
Work: depicts Luther pointing to a book as blood falls on a self-portrait of the artist, in its central panel
Work: its bottom panel depicts Luther in a crucifixion scene shared with his congregation
Work: a woodcut by Hans Brosamer caricaturing Luther with bees flying around one of seven heads
back: Caricature of Luther with Seven Heads (Hans Brosamer)

front: Work: a Hans Holbein the Younger caricature depicting Luther as a book-pointing Hercules trampling the dead bodies of Aristotle and four other philosophers
Figure: often depicted with a swan, due to a prophecy made at the Council of Constance
Figure: a Hans Baldung Grien woodcut depicting him as a saint inspired a lament by Albrecht Durer
Figure: a double portrait with his wife Katharina von Bora, who wears a fur coat, was created by his supporters after a hostile woodcut showed a mob storming a sheep shed
Concept: Erik Erikson's psychobiography Young Man Luther interprets Luther's religious crisis as a "second birth" identity crisis tied to his rejection of his father's plan for him to study law
Figure: vowed to Saint Anne during a thunderstorm that he would enter a monastery if he survived
Concept: Norman O. Brown's Life Against Death compares his theology's mysticism to Freudian psychoanalysis, noting his contemplation of the devil
Concept: Paul Tillich's autobiographical On the Boundary contrasts the effects of socialism on his life with the effects of Luther's ideas
Figure: his complete collected works are known as the Weimarer Ausgabe
